summary: Descended from Prince Sheng of Zhongshan but selling straw sandals and weaving mats when the Yellow Turban call comes. At 28 he reads the Zhuo volunteer notice and sighs at his helplessness — the sigh that draws Zhang Fei in. Initiates the Peach Garden oath, leads his first 500 troops to crush Cheng Yuanzhi, ambushes the rebels besieging Qingzhou, and joins Lu Zhi at Guangzong before being sent on to Yingchuan.

Wandering fugitive who slew a local bully five years earlier; arrives at the Zhuo inn as Liu Bei and Zhang Fei are drinking and becomes the middle of the three sworn brothers. Receives the hundred-pound Green-Dragon Saber and cleaves Cheng Yuanzhi in two in his first engagement.

Wine seller, butcher, and Zhuo farmer; overhears Liu Bei's sigh at the recruiting notice and proposes raising troops together. Hosts the Peach Garden oath, fights with the Serpent Halberd, kills Deng Mao at first pass, and has to be physically restrained from cutting down Dong Zhuo after the latter slights the brothers.

Introduced as Cavalry Commander, marching to support Huangfu Song and Zhu Jun at Yingchuan; intercepts the fleeing Yellow Turbans after the fire attack. The chapter folds in his biographical setup — feigned-fit prank against his uncle, Qiao Xuan and He Yong's prophecies, and Xu Shao's famous verdict 'in peace an able subject; in chaos a crafty hero'.

Imperial Protector of Xizhou; loses badly to Zhang Jue near Guangzong and is rescued by the Three Brothers, then treats them with such open rudeness that Zhang Fei tries to kill him on the spot. First appearance of the novel's recurring tyrant.

Wise and Worthy Master and Lord of Heaven; receives the Essential Arts of Peace from the Saint Hermit, builds thirty-six circuits of followers, and ignites the Yellow Turban Rebellion in the first month of Central Stability (AD 184). Routs Dong Zhuo near Guangzong before the Three Brothers' counter-charge.
Middle of the three Zhang brothers, styled Lord of Earth in the Yellow Turban uprising.
Youngest of the three Zhang brothers, styled Lord of Human (NOT the famous Han-founding strategist of the same name — pinyin collision).

Imperial Commander and Liu Bei's old teacher; besieging Zhang Jue at Guangzong with 50,000 troops. Refuses to bribe the inspecting eunuch Zuo Feng, is superseded by Dong Zhuo, and the Three Brothers encounter him being driven to the capital in a cage-cart.
Imperial Commander; with Zhu Jun crushes Zhang Bao and Zhang Liang at Yingchuan by setting fire to the Yellow Turban grass camp at night.
Imperial Commander; partners with Huangfu Song for the Yingchuan fire attack and pursues the fleeing rebels.
Imperial Protector of Youzhou and an Imperial-house scion; calls for volunteers, accepts the Three Brothers' first troop, and presents them to Liu Bei as a relation.
Commander under Liu Yan; advises raising volunteers, joins the Three Brothers' first sortie, and reinforces Liu Bei's relief of Qingzhou.
First Yellow Turban chieftain the Three Brothers face; cut in half by Guan Yu's Green-Dragon Saber.
Cheng Yuanzhi's lieutenant; killed in the opening clash by Zhang Fei's spear.
Imperial Protector of Qingzhou Region under siege; Liu Bei's relief column and ambush together break the besieging Yellow Turbans.
Horse merchant from Zhongshan fleeing the rebellion; gifts the Three Brothers fifty horses, 500 ounces of gold and silver, and 1,500 pounds of steel that become their first weapons.
Horse merchant partnered with Zhang Shiping; co-funder of the gift that arms the Three Brothers.
Liu Bei's uncle, who recognised him as no ordinary boy and kept the impoverished family from going hungry through childhood.
Renowned Han classical scholar; one of Liu Bei's two teachers in his youth.
Liu Bei's classmate under Zheng Xuan and Lu Zhi; mentioned here as a longstanding friend whose role will grow in later chapters.

Reigning Emperor Ling, whose neglect of court for the eunuchs frames the chapter. Receives the omens of the black serpent and the tempest, reads Cai Yong's remonstrance with a sigh, and dispatches Imperial Commanders against Zhang Jue's rebellion.
Emperor Huan, predecessor to Liu Hong; named only in the opening frame as the emperor who gave his confidence to the eunuchs.
Regent Marshal under Emperor Ling; with Chen Fan plots the destruction of the eunuchs and is killed when Cao Jie's counter-plot leaks the conspiracy.
Imperial Guardian; partners with Dou Wu against the eunuchs and dies with him when the plot is exposed.
Chief eunuch among the Ten Regular Attendants; so influential that the emperor calls him 'Foster Father'.
One of the Ten Regular Attendants — the cabal of eunuchs dominating the court.
One of the Ten Regular Attendants named in the opening roll.
One of the Ten Regular Attendants.
One of the Ten Regular Attendants; Zhang Jue writes letters to him hoping to plant a sympathiser inside the palace before the betrayal by Tang Zhou exposes the plot.
One of the Ten Regular Attendants.
One of the Ten Regular Attendants.
One of the Ten Regular Attendants; the chapter recounts how young Cao Cao earlier had his uncle beaten for breaching curfew.

Chief Eunuch in the prologue; foils Dou Wu and Chen Fan's plot, then engineers Cai Yong's expulsion when the Counselor blames the eunuchs for the omens. (Earlier-period figure; not the later Cao Jie of similar name.)
One of the Ten Regular Attendants.
Court Counselor who publicly tells Emperor Ling that the rainbows and crowing hens are caused by empresses and eunuchs meddling in state affairs; driven from court into rural exile by Cao Jie.
Regent Marshal; called on by Emperor Ling to suppress the Yellow Turban uprising. Arrests Ma Yuanyi and has him beheaded once Tang Zhou betrays the plot.
Native of Nanyang who tells young Cao Cao that only he can restore peace to the falling Han.
Elder statesman who tells young Cao Cao that rebellion is at hand and that he is the man to restore order.
Sage of Runan whom young Cao Cao consults about his future; delivers the famous verdict 'in peace you are an able subject; in chaos you are a crafty hero'.
Yellow Turban emissary sent to bribe the palace eunuchs; Tang Zhou betrays the plot and Ma Yuanyi is taken and beheaded before the uprising can be coordinated from inside the capital.
Yellow Turban entrusted with Zhang Jue's secret letter to Feng Xu; betrays the plot to the court, forcing Zhang Jue to launch the rebellion prematurely.
Cao Cao's father — born to the Xiahou family, adopted by the eunuch Cao Teng; cited in the chapter's biographical aside on Cao Cao's parentage.
Eunuch who adopted Cao Song, giving Cao Cao his nominal surname. Background detail in Cao Cao's introduction.
Daoist immortal calling himself Saint Hermit of the Southern Land; gives Zhang Jue the three volumes of the Essential Arts of Peace in the forest and vanishes into thin air.
The future Emperor Xian; not yet enthroned in this chapter and appears only in the broader frame about the Han's coming division into three parts.
Emperor Jing of the early Han (BC 157–141); Liu Bei's distant ancestor cited in the chapter's lineage paragraph.
Prince Sheng of Zhongshan, son of Emperor Jing and the founding ancestor of the line Liu Bei traces his descent through.
Han Supreme Ancestor and dynasty founder; cited in the chapter's opening frame ('slew a white serpent to raise the banners of uprising').
Founder of the Latter Han; opening-frame reference to the restoration of the empire after Wang Mang's rebellion.
Usurper between the Former and Latter Han; named in the opening frame setting up the dynasty's recurring instability.
Inspecting eunuch sent by the court to check on Lu Zhi at Guangzong; demands a bribe, takes offence at being refused, and engineers Lu Zhi's replacement by Dong Zhuo.
Minor figure mentioned in passing among the chapter's catalogue of names; no distinctive scene.
Minor lineage / court figure named only in passing during the chapter's frame.

After the Yellow Turbans are broken, Liu Bei is appointed magistrate of Anxi but is barely settled before the petty inspector arrives demanding a bribe. Refuses to abandon his ideals, restrains Zhang Fei after the whipping, and hangs his official seal on the inspector's neck before leaving with his brothers.
Restrains Zhang Fei from killing the abusive inspector outright, urging Liu Bei to give up the post rather than the man's life. Departs Anxi at his brother's side.
Erupts at the inspector's demand for a bribe and the threats against the locals — ties him to a hitching post and whips him bloody with a willow switch. Has to be talked down from finishing him off.

Newly elevated Regent Marshal as brother-in-law of Empress He; positions himself for the inevitable confrontation with the Ten Eunuchs after Emperor Ling's death. Receives the omens of his rise and begins gathering courtiers like Yuan Shao and Cao Cao around him; closes the chapter by sending out letters summoning regional armies to the capital — over the protests of Chen Lin and Cao Cao.

Emperor Ling, ailing throughout the chapter and dying on 13 May 189 after entrusting Crown Prince Bian to Jian Shuo. His death sets off the eunuch–Regent Marshal collision that drives the rest of the early novel.
Empress He; advances her son Liu Bian's claim, becomes Empress Dowager, and shields the Ten Eunuchs when He Jin proposes their destruction. Argues against bringing in outside armies.
Crown Prince Bian; thirteen-year-old son of Empress He; succeeds his father as Emperor Shao after Jian Shuo's coup attempt fails.
Liu Xie, second son of Emperor Ling by Consort Wang; favoured by Empress Dowager Dong and the eunuchs as an alternative to Bian. Survives the succession crisis as Prince of Chenliu.
Empress Dowager Dong; pushes her grandson Liu Xie's claim against Empress He's son Bian and is overruled when He Jin secures the succession.

Captain of the Western Garden; Emperor Ling entrusts him with Liu Xie's protection; conspires with the eunuchs to ambush He Jin at the deathbed audience. The plot leaks via Pan Yin and Jian Shuo is killed first.
Eunuch ally who leaks Jian Shuo's planned ambush of He Jin to the Regent Marshal, defusing the eunuch counter-coup.
Yuan Shao, scion of the Yuan clan; counsels He Jin to push the eunuchs to extinction and proposes summoning outside generals to force the issue.

Argues against He Jin's plan to summon outside armies — a single gaoler would be enough to deal with the eunuchs, he says. Walks out predicting that 'the one who throws the world into chaos is He Jin!'
Secretary to He Jin who pens the famous remonstrance against summoning outside armies — 'to cover the eyes and snatch at swallows is to fool oneself.' He Jin dismisses him.
Now Imperial Protector of Xizhou with 200,000 troops; rejoices at He Jin's summons to the capital and sets out with Li Jue, Guo Si, Zhang Ji, and Fan Chou.
Chief eunuch; conspires with Empress He to keep the eunuchs alive, then begins setting the trap that will spring on He Jin in the next chapter.
Eunuch; co-conspirator with Zhang Rang in the manoeuvring against He Jin.
Eunuch; among the ten conspiring against He Jin.
Eunuch; among the conspirators.
Eunuch; among the conspirators.
Dong Chong, Empress Dowager Dong's nephew; killed when He Jin removes the Dong family's military commands.
Imperial Commander celebrated for the Yingchuan victory but jailed and stripped of rank when he refuses to bribe Zhao Zhong. Released after Huangfu Song's officers intervene.
Imperial Commander; released and restored after Huangfu Song-style intercessions; quietly retires as the political climate sours.
Imperial Commander; pursues remnant Yellow Turbans and crushes Han Zhong at Wancheng, ending the main rebellion in this chapter.
Yellow Turban remnant chief at Wancheng; killed by Zhu Jun after surrendering.
Sun Jian of Wu; arrives with his Wu troops to fight at Wancheng and is rewarded with the governorship of Changsha — his first major appointment in the novel.
Mentioned among the regional governors whose lists are being passed around as He Jin gathers allies.
Liu Yu, listed among the regional governors being considered by He Jin.
Yuan Wei, uncle of Yuan Shao; among He Jin's senior advisers.
Minister who joins the chorus warning He Jin not to summon outside armies.
Xun You; named among the courtiers being drawn into He Jin's camp.
Court official among He Jin's circle as the eunuch crisis builds.
Prince Sheng of Zhongshan — Liu Bei's distant ancestor named in passing as the chapter recapitulates Liu Bei's lineage.
Emperor Huan; referenced in the chapter's recap of the lead-up to Emperor Ling's death.
Chen Fan; referenced in the chapter's frame about the earlier eunuch–minister collision.
Zhang Liang the Yellow Turban; mentioned in passing as the rebellion is wound down.
Zhang Bao the Yellow Turban; mentioned in passing as the rebellion is wound down (NOT Zhang Fei's later cousin Zhang Bao).
Zhang Jue; referenced as already dead by chapter's start — the rebellion he sparked is being mopped up.
Han founder; named in the chapter's recap of dynastic background.
He Miao, He Jin's brother; lukewarm ally drawn into the eunuch confrontation.
Minor courtier named in passing during the He Jin / eunuch manoeuvres.
Liu Xian; minor imperial-clan figure named in passing.
Emperor Chong (143–15 Feb 145); cited in a historical aside about short-lived child emperors of the Han.
Court figure named in passing.
Court figure named in passing.
Court figure named in passing.
Court lady named in passing as part of imperial-household backstory.
Yuan Feng; minor courtier in the Yuan clan.
Court figure named in passing.
Court figure named in passing (NOT Sun Tzu the strategist of warfare — pinyin collision).
Minor imperial-clan figure named in passing.
Minor court figure named in passing.
Yellow Turban remnant chief at Wancheng; killed in the suppression.
Lady of Wuyang; minor noblewoman named in passing in the court backstory.
Chen Dan; loyal minister killed by the eunuchs in the recap of earlier court purges.
Yellow Turban general named in the chapter's mop-up; killed in passing.
Minor figure named in passing during the Yellow Turban mop-up.
Liu Hu; minor imperial-clan figure named in passing.
Liu Tao; minor imperial-clan figure named in passing.
Zang Min; minor figure named in passing.
Zhang Chun; remnant rebel chief whose Liang-Province uprising is suppressed during the chapter.
Zhang Ju; minor rebel named in passing.
Zhang Jun; minor figure named in passing.
Zhao Hong; remnant rebel chief named in passing.

Arrives at the capital with his Xiliang army just as the eunuchs have killed He Jin, and walks straight into the resulting power vacuum. Adopts the boy Emperor Bian and the Prince of Chenliu, browbeats the court, executes Ding Yuan via Lu Bu's defection, and deposes Bian in favour of Liu Xie (Emperor Xian).

Lu Bu, Ding Yuan's adopted son and lieutenant; corrupted by Li Su with the warhorse Red Hare, gold and pearls. Murders Ding Yuan in his tent, presents the head to Dong Zhuo, and is taken as Dong Zhuo's new adopted son — the betrayal that defines his arc.
Ding Yuan, Imperial Protector of Bingzhou and adoptive father of Lu Bu; objects publicly to Dong Zhuo's plan to depose the emperor and is murdered by Lu Bu the following night.
Li Su, Dong Zhuo's envoy; bribes Lu Bu with Red Hare, gold, pearls, and a peerless place at Dong Zhuo's table — the gift that buys Lu Bu's father-killing defection.

Li Ru, Dong Zhuo's son-in-law and chief adviser; engineers the deposition plot, restrains Dong Zhuo from killing Yuan Shao at the banquet, and reads out the deposition document on the first of the ninth month.
Faces Dong Zhuo at the banquet, draws his sword on equal terms, hangs his official seals on the East Gate, and rides away to Bohai — beginning his career as the eastern coalition's nominal leader.
At the same banquet quietly steps back; survives Dong Zhuo's purge by playing along while he plans the assassination attempt that closes the next chapter.
Walks into the Palace of Happiness despite Chen Lin's warning and is cut down by the eunuchs at the Gate of Grand Virtue — the slaughter that triggers Yuan Shao's massacre of every eunuch in Luoyang.
Leader of the Ten Eunuchs; engineers He Jin's murder and dies in the chaos when Yuan Shao and Yuan Shu storm the palace.
Emperor Bian (Liu Bian); spirited out of Luoyang during the eunuch massacre, hides in a marsh, is found by Min Gong and the Imperial Protectors, and is brought back only to be deposed by Dong Zhuo.
(see Liu Hong / Liu Bian — same emperor here under his prior name; deposed and renamed Prince of Hongnong by Dong Zhuo this chapter.)
Liu Xie, Prince of Chenliu; nine years old, calm and articulate when found in the marsh — the steadiness Dong Zhuo cites when he installs him as Emperor Xian.
Empress Dowager Dong (no relation to Dong Zhuo); demoted to Empress He and confined; the new regime moves quickly to neutralise the He clan.
Empress He; stripped of her royal robes and confined with Bian and Consort Tang in the Palace of Forever Calm.
Li Jue; one of Dong Zhuo's four named generals (with Guo Si, Zhang Ji, Fan Chou) — first appearance of the band that will inherit power after Dong Zhuo's death.
Guo Si; one of Dong Zhuo's four generals.
Zhang Ji; one of Dong Zhuo's four generals.
Fan Chou; one of Dong Zhuo's four generals.
Lu Zhi alone stands against Dong Zhuo at the council and warns 'in appearance innocent, he is a very wolf at heart'; resigns when Dong Zhuo refuses to listen.
Zheng Tai; resigns alongside Lu Zhi rather than serve Dong Zhuo.
Yang Biao; senior minister silenced by Dong Zhuo's sword at the banquet.
Yuan Shu; joins his cousin in storming the palace and butchering the eunuchs.
Min Gong; finds the lost emperor and the Prince of Chenliu in the reedy marsh and escorts them back toward the capital.
Peng Bo; rides with Min Gong in the search for the missing princes.
Dong Min, Dong Zhuo's younger brother; given the lordship of Hu and command of the Left Army when Dong Zhuo distributes the spoils.
Niu Fu, Dong Zhuo's son-in-law; left behind to govern Xizhou when Dong Zhuo marches east.
Chunyu Qiong; one of the courtiers caught up in the eunuch massacre's aftermath.
Wu Kuang; partisan in the palace fighting against the eunuchs.
He Miao; killed in the chaos of the eunuch coup against He Jin.
Eunuch killed in Yuan Shao's massacre.
Eunuch killed in Yuan Shao's massacre.
Eunuch killed in Yuan Shao's massacre.
Eunuch killed in Yuan Shao's massacre.
Eunuch killed in Yuan Shao's massacre.
Eunuch (earlier-era figure); named in passing in the chapter's recap.
Cai Yong; recalled from exile by Dong Zhuo to give the new regime the gloss of legitimacy.
Bao Xin; warns Yuan Shao after the deposition banquet that they should strike Dong Zhuo while he can be struck — ignored, he withdraws to Taishan.
Eunuch (Ten Regular Attendants); killed in the palace massacre.
Cui Lie; minor official mentioned in passing.
Cui Yi; minor courtier named in passing.
Zhao Meng; minor courtier named in passing.
Tai Jia; historical Shang-dynasty reference cited by Dong Zhuo to justify deposing an emperor.
Yi Yin; ancient minister cited as the model for Dong Zhuo's deposition speech.
Huo Guang; Western Han regent cited as the other historical precedent Dong Zhuo invokes.
Liu He; minor imperial-clan figure named in passing.

Completes the deposition on the first of the ninth month, has Emperor Bian, Empress He and Consort Tang murdered, and begins his reign of terror at court — until Cao Cao's failed knife attempt forces him to issue a national warrant.

Offers Wang Yun the 'seven-jewelled sword' to assassinate Dong Zhuo; bungles the strike when Dong Zhuo turns in his mirror at the wrong moment and improvises by presenting the sword as a gift. Flees on a borrowed horse, is captured by Chen Gong at Zhongmou, talks his way free, and commits the chapter's defining crime — slaying the Lu Boshe household on suspicion, then killing Lu Boshe himself with 'rather we let down the world than the world let us down'.

Chen Gong, magistrate of Zhongmou; captures the fleeing Cao Cao, is moved by his patriotism, abandons his post to ride with him — and is so revolted by the Lu Boshe killings that he rides away in silence before dawn.
Lu Boshe, Cao Cao's father's sworn brother; hosts the fugitive Cao Cao and rides out to buy wine for him. Returns home to find his family slaughtered and is cut down by Cao Cao on the road.
Lu Bu; mostly a looming presence as Dong Zhuo's bodyguard. His proximity is the reason Cao Cao misses his strike — the assassin freezes when Lu Bu returns with horses.
Li Ru; advises Dong Zhuo through the deposition and Cao Cao's escape, pushes the murder of Empress He and Consort Tang, and engineers the national hue and cry for Cao Cao.
Wang Yun; pledges the seven-jewelled sword to Cao Cao at the secret banquet — first link in the chain of plots that will culminate in Lu Bu's chained scheme.
Liu Bian, formerly Emperor Shao, now Prince of Hongnong; locked away in the Palace of Forever Calm with his mother and consort, then murdered by Li Ru's poison.
Empress He; thrown from a high tower on Li Ru's orders, on the same day as her son's poisoning.
Consort Tang; pleads to die with Liu Bian and is strangled with her own silken scarf — the most affecting moment of the deposed emperor's death.
Emperor Xian; nine years old and freshly enthroned, watches the chapter from behind the curtain as Dong Zhuo's regime hardens. Receives Cao Cao's failed strike from across the audience hall.
Ding Guan, Chair of the Secretariat; objects to the deposition with his ivory baton and is summarily executed — railing at Dong Zhuo even on the way to the block.
Yuan Wei; rebuked by Dong Zhuo for his nephew's defiance, kowtows to spare the Yuans from immediate proscription.
Yuan Shao; appears off-stage — Dong Zhuo lifts the death sentence on him and grants him Bohai on Zhou Bi and Wu Qiong's advice.
Zhou Bi, court counselor; counsels Dong Zhuo to pardon Yuan Shao and the Yuan clan rather than provoke them.
Wu Qiong, Commander; advises with Zhou Bi to give Yuan Shao a posting and disarm him through rank rather than force.
Wu Fu; minor courtier named in passing during the deposition banquet.
Cai Yong; recalled by Dong Zhuo to lend the new regime literary respectability.
Liu Hong; framed as the deceased emperor whose succession Dong Zhuo is overturning.
He Jin; named in passing as the now-dead Regent Marshal whose summons brought Dong Zhuo into Luoyang.

After fleeing to Qiao he raises the call to arms 'Loyalty and Honor', burns through Wei Hong's wealth to equip volunteers, and gathers his first lieutenants — Yue Jin, Li Dian, Xiahou Dun, Xiahou Yuan, Cao Ren, Cao Hong. Drafts the manifesto that brings the Eighteen Lords' coalition into being at Bohai.

Convenes the coalition of eighteen at Bohai with 30,000 troops; takes the oath as Allied Commander; the rest of the chapter watches him struggle to coordinate the lords until Hua Xiong's killing spree at Sishui Pass forces decisive action.

Brings Guan Yu and Zhang Fei to the coalition under Gongsun Zan's wing; mocked by Yuan Shu for his low station until Cao Cao defends him. Watches helplessly as the named lords' champions fall one after another to Hua Xiong, then says nothing while Guan Yu offers.

Steps forward at the coalition tent after Hua Xiong has cut down Yu She and Pan Feng. Promises to return before the wine that Cao Cao pours him is cold — and does, dropping Hua Xiong's head on the table with the cup still warm. The chapter's signature scene.

After Guan Yu's victory, joins his brothers in the famous three-against-one melee at Hulao Pass, hurling Lu Bu back as the Three Brothers together fight him to a draw — the most-quoted set piece in early RotK.

Lu Bu in his coming-out chapter; cleaves through the coalition's named champions before being held in check by all three of Liu Bei, Guan Yu, and Zhang Fei together. Withdraws to Hulao Pass under his lord's umbrella, dropping Mu Shun and Wu Anguo and shattering Fang Yue along the way.
Hua Xiong; Dong Zhuo's pass-defender at Sishui who slaughters Bao Zhong, Yu She, Pan Feng — and is finally killed by Guan Yu in 'the time it takes wine to cool.'
Dispatches Hua Xiong and then Lu Bu to hold Sishui Pass and Hulao Pass against the coalition; remains in Luoyang as the news arrives that the eastern alliance is real.
Cao Hong; one of Cao Cao's two cousins to arrive at Qiao with 1,000 followers each at the call to arms.
Cao Ren; the other of Cao Cao's two cousins arriving at Qiao.
Xiahou Dun; arrives with his cousin Xiahou Yuan and a thousand trained soldiers each, becoming part of Cao Cao's personal staff.
Xiahou Yuan; arrives with Xiahou Dun and joins Cao Cao's personal staff.
Yue Jin; one of Cao Cao's first volunteer captains.
Li Dian; arrives from Julu among the first to join Cao Cao.
Wei Hong; graduate at Qiao whose family wealth Cao Cao taps to equip the volunteer army.
Yuan Shu; Governor of Nanyang; in charge of the coalition's commissariat — and refuses Sun Jian supplies, leading to the chapter's other major friction.
Sun Jian; coalition van leader who breaks Sishui Pass open until Yuan Shu cuts off his grain and Hua Xiong counter-attacks. Loses Zu Mao in the rout but escapes the pass to fight again.
Zu Mao; Sun Jian's general who swaps helmets with him in the rout from Sishui Pass and is killed luring Hua Xiong's troops away — saving Sun Jian's life.
Huang Gai; serves under Sun Jian at Sishui Pass.
Cheng Pu; serves under Sun Jian at Sishui Pass.
Han Dang; serves under Sun Jian at Sishui Pass.
Gongsun Zan; brings his Beiping forces into the coalition with Liu Bei, Guan Yu and Zhang Fei in tow, and presents the Three Brothers to Yuan Shao.
Kong Rong; among the named lords joining the coalition (Governor of Beihai).
Kong Zhou; Imperial Protector of Yuzhou; among the named coalition members.
Liu Dai; Imperial Protector of Yanzhou; coalition member.
Zhang Chao; Governor of Guangling; coalition member.
Zhang Miao; Governor of Chenliu; coalition member.
Zhang Yang; Governor of Shangdang; coalition member.
Qiao Mao; Governor of Dongjun; coalition member.
Ma Teng; the Xiliang governor; pledged to the coalition through letters from the west.
Tao Qian; Imperial Protector of Xuzhou; pledged to the coalition.
Wang Kuang; Governor of Hanei; coalition member — one of the first to send champions against Hua Xiong (his man Fang Yue is killed).
Han Fu; Imperial Protector of Jizhou; coalition member providing logistical grain.
Li Feng; an officer in the coalition's commissariat.
Fang Yue; Wang Kuang's champion who is the first lord-named warrior cut down by Lu Bu outside Hulao Pass.
Mu Shun; Zhang Yang's champion; killed by Lu Bu in the Hulao Pass fighting.
Wu Anguo; Kong Rong's champion; loses his hand to Lu Bu before being rescued by his comrades.
Bao Zhong; Bao Xin's brother; killed by Hua Xiong at Sishui Pass — first major coalition casualty.
Bao Xin; jealous over the front-line honour, sends his brother Bao Zhong out ahead of orders; sees Bao Zhong killed.
Zhao Cen; Yuan Shu's officer; among coalition staff.
Pan Feng; Han Fu's champion; killed by Hua Xiong at Sishui Pass.
Yu She; Yuan Shu's champion; killed by Hua Xiong at Sishui Pass before Guan Yu steps forward.
Xiahou Ying; named in passing as the ancient ancestor from whom Xiahou Dun descends.
Hu Zhen; Dong Zhuo's general at Sishui Pass under Hua Xiong; routed when the Three Brothers break through.
Li Jue; among Dong Zhuo's named generals defending the western approach.
Guo Si; among Dong Zhuo's named generals.
Fan Chou; among Dong Zhuo's named generals.
Zhang Ji; among Dong Zhuo's named generals.
Li Su; among Dong Zhuo's inner circle following Lu Bu's defection.
Li Ru; counsels Dong Zhuo to retreat to Changan when Sishui falls — the seed of the next chapter's capital relocation.
Emperor Ling; named only as the dead emperor whose chaos this chapter is winding down.
Emperor Xian; in nominal possession of the throne while the coalition fights to liberate him.
The deposed Liu Bian; named in passing in the recap of why the coalition has formed.
Yuan Wei; named in passing — Dong Zhuo wipes out the Yuan clan in Luoyang in retaliation for Yuan Shao's coalition.
Yuan Yi; named in passing among the Yuan clan victims of Dong Zhuo's purge.
Yan Liang; named among the warriors in Yuan Shao's retinue (his decisive role comes later, at Baima).
Wen Chou; named among Yuan Shao's warriors (his role expands at the Yan Ford in chapter 26).
Emperor Huan; named in passing in the chapter's frame.
He Yong; minister opposed to summoning outside generals; aligns with Yuan Shao but warns He Jin against the regional-armies plan.
Dou Wu; named in passing during the chapter's recap of the earlier failed eunuch-purge plot.
Chen Lin; warns He Jin against entering the Palace of Happiness on the day Zhang Rang's ambush is waiting; ignored, the warning proves correct moments later.
Wang Yun; survives the chapter's purge and the deposition banquet — quietly biding his time for the chained scheme that will arrive in chapter 8.
Chen Gong; named in passing in this chapter — Cao Cao still has the Zhongmou magistrate's loyalty after the Lu Boshe killings of chapter 4.
